```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "niche_genome": "Assumption: ground wheeled mobile robots such as differential-drive, skid-steer, or Ackermann platforms doing trajectory tracking or navigation in moderately structured environments.",
    "objective_genome": "Improve closed-loop stability, tracking accuracy, robustness to disturbances, smoothness of motion, energy efficiency, and real-time computational performance.",
    "control_methods_genome": [
      "Use PID only for simple low-speed systems; tune with anti-windup and feedforward terms.",
      "Use LQR or LQG for linearized dynamics when operating near nominal trajectories.",
      "Use Model Predictive Control, especially nonlinear MPC, for handling constraints, actuator limits, collision-avoidance coupling, and performance optimization.",
      "Use robust control such as H-infinity, sliding mode, or tube MPC when model uncertainty, wheel slip, or payload changes are significant.",
      "Use adaptive control when robot parameters vary over time, such as changing mass, terrain friction, or battery voltage.",
      "Use control Lyapunov functions and control barrier functions when formal stability and safety guarantees are required.",
      "Use learning-based control cautiously, preferably as residual learning or policy improvement on top of a stable model-based controller."
    ],
    "modeling_estimation_genome": [
      "Improve performance by identifying accurate kinematic and dynamic models, including actuator delay, saturation, friction, and wheel slip.",
      "Use sensor fusion with EKF, UKF, factor graphs, or visual-inertial odometry to reduce state-estimation error.",
      "Account for latency and discretization effects, since poor timing can destabilize otherwise well-designed controllers.",
      "Use trajectory smoothing and dynamically feasible reference generation to avoid asking the controller to track impossible commands."
    ],
    "validation_metrics_genome": [
      "Stability margins or Lyapunov-based guarantees",
      "RMS and maximum tracking error",
      "Settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error",
      "Control effort and energy consumption",
      "Robustness under terrain changes, payload changes, sensor noise, and actuator saturation",
      "Real-time feasibility: control-loop frequency, latency, CPU/GPU load",
      "Safety metrics such as constraint violations, collisions, and emergency stops"
    ],
    "limitation_genome": "This recommendation does not directly cover aerial robots, legged robots, underwater robots, high-speed autonomous racing, or manipulation-heavy mobile platforms; those domains require different stability models and performance tradeoffs."

}
```

[result] Literature consensus: Pt remains the benchmark, especially in acidic PEM conditions. Non-noble catalysts such as NiMo alloys, MoS₂ edge-site catalysts, transition-metal phosphides/carbides/nitrides, and sulfides can approach Pt-like activity in selected conditions, especially alkaline media, but often face durability, conductivity, mass-loading, and high-current-density challenges. Representative literature includes Hinnemann et al., JACS 2005; Jaramillo et al., Science 2007; McKone et al., Energy Environ. Sci. 2011; Popczun et al., JACS 2013; Kibsgaard et al., Nat. Chem. 2014; Vesborg and Jaramillo, RSC Adv. 2012.

```action
    "evidence_genome": "Evidence from the literature supports strong non-noble HER performance in specific catalyst classes. MoS₂ was identified as an active HER catalyst through edge-site sulfur chemistry, with Hinnemann et al. proposing MoS₂ edge sites as biomimetic HER centers and Jaramillo et al., Science 2007, experimentally correlating HER activity with MoS₂ edge-site density. NiMo catalysts have shown high alkaline HER activity; McKone et al., Energy Environ. Sci. 2011, reported nickel–molybdenum nanopowders with strong performance in alkaline electrolyte. Transition-metal phosphides such as Ni₂P also exhibit high HER activity; Popczun et al., JACS 2013, showed nanostructured Ni₂P as an efficient HER electrocatalyst. Reviews such as Vesborg and Jaramillo, RSC Adv. 2012, and later work on sulfides, phosphides, carbides, nitrides, and alloys show that non-noble catalysts can reduce overpotentials substantially compared with bare Ni, steel, or carbon supports.",
    "comparison_to_platinum_genome": "Pt remains the universal HER benchmark because it combines near-optimal hydrogen binding energy, very low overpotential, high exchange current density, broad pH activity, high conductivity, and strong durability, especially in acidic media.     "limitation_genome": "This evaluation does not claim that all non-noble catalysts can replace Pt in all HER devices. }
```

Final proposal: Under this assumption, the comparison would likely conclude that many simple vacancies in lead-halide perovskites, especially halide vacancies V_X and A-site vacancies V_A, tend to have relatively low formation energies but often produce shallow electronic levels, so they are not always the dominant recombination-active defects. These deep defects can act as nonradiative recombination centers and reduce open-circuit voltage and carrier lifetime.
